person.name: Sybil de Mandeville
person.userCertifiedLifeSummary:
«b»Biography«/b»
Sybil de Mandeville was the daughter an unknown de Mandeville and Alice Giffard, and a niece of Walter Giffard, bishop of archbishop of York and Godfrey Giffard, bishop of Worcester. Alice (Giffard) Mandeville was apparently the "favorite sister" of Walter Giffard based on the multiple gifts to her found in his Register. Bishop Godfrey Giffard named 'lady Sybil de Bodaringham, my niece' in his will of 1302. Sybil was born 29 September 1264 as she was 24 and more on the Feast of St. Michael last in the November 1288 IPM of her brother. The name of her father remains uncertain. In the 1288 IPM in Bedford, Sybil was found to be the heir of her brother Walter de Mandeveille's 1/6 share of the manor of Lutton, co. Bedford; Godfrey, bishop of Worcester claimed the manor of Alerynton (Alkerton), co. Oxford which he said he gave to Walter de Mandeville with the provision it should revert back to him if Walter died without heirs of his body. As a clue to the ancestry of Sybil and Walter, Walter had previously held the portions of the manors of Eltham, Woolwich and Mottingham, however he exchanged these for 1/6 of the manor of Luton with John de Vesci. One half of Eltham was anciently given to the Mandeville family but its exact descent to Walter de Mandeville is not known. However, in 1255 John de Marisco answered for the fee of Woolwich under Ralph de Mandeville. It seems a good possibility that Sybil and Walter are the children of this Ralph de Mandeville. Though at least one web site makes them the children of was Edmund de Mandeville citing 'A Survey of the Cathedral Church of Worcester. Neither this Ralph nor Edmund have yet been identified in the main Mandeville family as of yet.

Sybil was a lady of the Queen's chamber in the retinue of Eleanor of Castile, but whether she held this position before or after the death of her first husband is not certain. She perhaps became known to the queen as her family held one half of Eltham, while the other half was royal demesne and the site of the royal palace at Eltham. In 1270, Henry III "kept a public Christmas at his palace of Eltham, being accompanied by the Queen, and all the great men of the realm." Sybil is frequently found in the household records of the Queen Eleanor beginning in February 1286, though an earlier connection to the Queen may be indicated by her marriage to Peter Le Poer. The Le Poers were 'Barons of Dunnoil' derived from their chief manor of Dunhill, co. Waterford, Ireland, and Sybil's son would hold this title. In 1274, Queen Eleanor was granted the wardship and marriage of the heir of John Le Poer. Sybil was subsequently married to Peter Le Poer and by him had a son John born 29 August 1281 at Caernarvon, Wales. From John's proof of age we also learn that Peter Le Poer drowned at sea on 4 April 1283.

Sybil married Henry de Bodrugan sometime before the IPM of her brother Walter de Mandville was taken on 6 November 1288. Henry de Bodrugan was in the expedition to Gascony in 1286 with King Edward, which was also accompanied by Queen Eleanor and a large number of the royal household. It is possible that it was while traveling with the king and queen that the match with Sybil was made. Queen Eleanor was well known for matchmatching of cousins and women of the queen's household to well landed English nobles. It is also possible that the Bodrugans strong religious connections played a role (marrying the niece of two powerful bishops to a major benefactor of Glasney College and the brother of the archdeacon of Cornwall). The first child of Sybil and Henry was born 6 January 1290 at Bodrugan, co. Cornwall. It may be that Sybil remained in the household of Queen Eleanor for a time after her second marriage as Sybil's children were still with the queen's children in 1289-90. Eleanor died in November 1290 and her will includes a bequest "to Sybil, wife of Henry de Boderingeham, of the marriage of John le Power, son and heir of Peter le Power, tenant in chief, her former husband." 

It seems likely that it is Sybil's connections to Ireland possibly through the Mandevilles (important tenants of the earl of Ulster) and certainly through the Le Poers that Henry de Bodrugan is found with apparently new Irish interests. On 12 April 1300 he had letters nominating John Le Fuelere as his attorney in Ireland for two years. And again on 2 February 1301 similar letters were issued nominating his brothers John and Peter Bodrugan as his attorneys and then on 5 April 1301 nominating John Bodrugan and John le Foghelere. In January 1305, Sybil's son 'John Le Poher baron of Doneyl' was found to owe a debt of L200 to Henry de Bodrugan which John acknowledged. The sheriff was commanded to seize the goods of John and put them up for sale in order to pay the debt. The sheriff took 2 stacks of wheat, two oats, 30 oxen 20 heifers, 60 cows, and 200 sheep; however, when the goods were offered for sale "no one in the county dared to buy them." The sheriff again tried in May 1305 to collect the debt with a similar result.

Henry de Bodrugan died shortly before 23 January 1309 when a writ of diem clausit extremum was issued. He was last noted living on 29 October 1308 when Henry presented at St. Martins-by-Looe. His IPM shows him holding the manors Bodrugan, Pendrym, Trethem, Tretheak, Treworrick, Tremodret, Trevellion, Tregrehan, Trethew and Lantyan Parva. The large manor of Restronguet does not appear as it was still held by his mother who did not die until 1311. He was lord of the borough of Looe in his manor of Pendrim. In addition to these manors, his IPM lists 100 messuages, 40 tofts, 12 mills, 6 dovecotes (pigeon housing), 100 gardens, 60 Cornish acres, 400 acres of meadow, 1000 acres of pasture, 60 acres wood, 1000 acres of heath (open uncultivated land), and was collecting rent from land in 20 other manors. He also held the advowsons of Mevagissey, St. Martin-by-Looe, Duloe and Poundstock.

Sybil was last noted living in the May 1305 dispute with her son John Le Poer, and had died prior to the IPM of her husband on 23 January 1309. In the Bedford inquisition of Henry de Bodrugan, the 1/6 share of Lutton (inherited from her brother) was found to belong to John Le Poer, son of Henry's wife Sybil which Henry had held by the courtesy of England. In fact all Mandeville estates would have gone to John Le Poer and the best chance of determining her ancestry may be to see if any other Mandeville estates were inherited by the Le Poer's. Sybil was buried within the church of Glasney College where an alter was especially endowed for her.

zh.St Endellion, Cornwall, England: 英國可瓦爾聖 恩德利
zh.lifeSummary: 當Sybil de Mandeville於1264年9月29日在英國貝德福爾盧頓受洗時，她的父親Walter De Mandeville55歲，母親Alice Giffard25歲。她於大約1280年與Peter le Poer結婚。他們至少是1個兒子的父母。她去世於大約1307年在英國可瓦爾瑞享年44歲，埋葬在英國可瓦爾瑞。

zh-hans.St Endellion, Cornwall, England: 英国可瓦爾聖 恩德利
zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Sybil de Mandeville于1264年9月29日在英国貝德福爾盧頓受洗时，她的父亲Walter De Mandeville55岁，母亲Alice Giffard25岁。她于大约1280年与Peter le Poer结婚。他们至少是1个儿子的父母。她去世于大约1307年在英国可瓦爾瑞享年44岁，埋葬在英国可瓦爾瑞。

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Mandeville
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Marcel, Andre, Armand, Gabrielle, Camille, Emile, Fernand, Fernande, Gaston, Jean-Paul, Lucien, Michel.

French: habitational name from Mandeville, the name of two places in Normandy, derived from Latin magna villa ‘large estate’, or a variant of Manneville, a habitational name from Manneville, the name of several places in Normandy (see 2 below). Compare Montville and Monville .
English (of Norman origin): habitational name from Manneville (Seine-Maritime, Calvados), Manneville-sur-Risle (Eure), or Magneville (Manche).

variants.name: Mansfield
meanings: English: habitational name from a place so named in Nottinghamshire. The placename means ‘open land by the river Maun’, from the river name Maun (which may take its name from a hill named with Old English mamme ‘teat’) + Old English feld ‘open country’.
Irish (Waterford): when not an importation of 1, this is an altered form of the Norman name de Manville (see Mandeville 3).
Americanized form of German and Jewish (Ashkenazic) Mansfeld, a habitational name from a place so called in Saxony.

variants.name: Manderville
meanings: English (of Norman origin): variant of   Mandeville  .

variants.name: Manwell
meanings: English and Scottish: variant of   Mandeville  .

variants.name: Manville
meanings: English (Sussex and Lancashire): variant of   Mandeville  .
Altered form of   Mainville   1, a surname of Swiss French origin (see   Miville  ).
French: variant of Menville, a habitational name from Menville in Haute-Garonne, and also of Mainville 2.

variants.name: Mundell
meanings: Scottish (of Norman origin): variant of   Mandeville  .
Americanized form of German Mündel (or some other similar, like-sounding surname): nickname for someone with a prominent mouth, from Middle High German munt, mündelīn ‘(little) mouth’.

variants.name: Monville
meanings: Altered form of French   Mandeville  .
Walloon: habitational name from Monville in Namur province in Wallonia (Belgium), of the same etymology as in 3 below.
In some cases possibly also French: habitational name from Montville in Seine-Maritime or Monville in Calvados, both named with Old French mont ‘mountain, hill’ + ville ‘settlement’.

variants.name: Mandal
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Chandi, Sanjay, Sankar, Somnath, Aditi, Aditya, Ajit, Amala, Amitabha, Anil, Anirudh, Aparna.
Indian (northern states): from Sanskrit maṇḍala, a word that has several meanings, including ‘sphere’ and ‘halo’.
English (Cumbria; of Norman origin): variant of   Mandeville  .

variants.name: Montville
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Emile, Vernice.
Altered form of French   Mandeville  .
French: variant of   Monville  . This surname is very rare in France, found mainly in Creuse.

variants.name: Manfull
meanings: English (Midlands): probably a variant of   Mandeville  . Compare   Manville  .

nameMeanings.name: Sybil
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From the name (Greek Sibylla or Sybilla, with confusion over the vowels from an early period) of a class of ancient prophetesses inspired by Apollo. According to medieval theology, they were pagans denied the knowledge of Christ but blessed by God with some insight into things to come and accordingly admitted to heaven. It was thus regarded as a respectable name to be borne by Christians. This name was introduced to England by the Normans and enjoyed considerable popularity in the Middle Ages.

variants.name: Sybilla
meanings: From the name (Greek Sibylla or Sybilla, with confusion over the vowels from an early period) of a class of ancient prophetesses inspired by Apollo. According to medieval theology, they were pagans denied the knowledge of Christ but blessed by God with some insight into things to come and accordingly admitted to heaven. It was thus regarded as a respectable name to be borne by Christians. This name was introduced to England by the Normans and enjoyed considerable popularity in the Middle Ages.

variants.name: Sibilla
meanings: From the name (Greek Sibylla or Sybilla, with confusion over the vowels from an early period) of a class of ancient prophetesses inspired by Apollo. According to medieval theology, they were pagans denied the knowledge of Christ but blessed by God with some insight into things to come and accordingly admitted to heaven. It was thus regarded as a respectable name to be borne by Christians. This name was introduced to England by the Normans and enjoyed considerable popularity in the Middle Ages.

variants.name: Sybille
meanings: From Greek. See Sybil .

variants.name: Sibella
meanings: From the name (Greek Sibylla or Sybilla, with confusion over the vowels from an early period) of a class of ancient prophetesses inspired by Apollo. According to medieval theology, they were pagans denied the knowledge of Christ but blessed by God with some insight into things to come and accordingly admitted to heaven. It was thus regarded as a respectable name to be borne by Christians. This name was introduced to England by the Normans and enjoyed considerable popularity in the Middle Ages.

historicEvents.title: Jews Expelled From England
historicEvents.description: After years of anti-Semitic behavior, King Edward I expelled Jews from England in 1290.

en.lifeSummary: When Sybil de Mandeville was christened on 29 September 1264, in Luton, Bedfordshire, England, her father, Walter De Mandeville, was 55 and her mother, Alice Giffard, was 25. She married Peter le Poer about 1280. They were the parents of at least 1 son. She died about 1307, in Penryn, Cornwall, England, at the age of 44, and was buried in Penryn, Cornwall, England.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Sybil de Mandeville a été baptisée le 29 septembre 1264, à Luton, Bedfordshire, Angleterre, son père, Walter De Mandeville, avait 55 ans et sa mère, Alice Giffard, avait 25 ans. Elle a épousé Peter le Poer environ 1280. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils. Elle est décédée environ 1307, à Penryn, Cornouailles, Angleterre, à l’âge de 44 ans, et a été enterrée à Penryn, Cornouailles, Angleterre.

de.lifeSummary: Als Sybil de Mandeville am 29. September 1264 in Luton, Bedfordshire, England getauft wurde, war ihr Vater Walter De Mandeville 55 und ihre Mutter Alice Giffard 25. Sie hat um 1280 Peter le Poer geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 1 Sohn. Sie starb um 1307 im Alter von 44 Jahren in Penryn, Cornwall, England und wurde in Penryn, Cornwall, England begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Sybil de Mandeville è stato battezzato il 29 settembre 1264 nel Luton, Bedfordshire, England, sua padre, Walter De Mandeville, aveva 55 anni e sua madre, Alice Giffard, aveva 25 anni. Lei sposato Peter le Poer circa 1280. Erano genitori di almeno 1 figlio. Morì circa 1307, nel Penryn, Cornwall, England, all'età di 44 anni, e fu sepolta a Penryn, Cornwall, England.

ja.lifeSummary: Sybil de Mandevilleが1264年9月29日にイギリスベドフォードシュアイアルトンで洗礼を受けた時，父Walter De Mandevilleは55歳，母Alice Giffardは25歳であった。約1280年にPeter le Poerと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも1人の息子の親であった。彼女は約1307年にイギリスコルンワルペンリンで44歳で死去し，イギリスコルンワルペンリンにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Sybil de Mandeville(이)가 1264년 9월 29일 영국 베드퍼드실르 루턴에서 침례를 받았을 때, 그녀의 아버지 Walter De Mandeville(은)는 55세였고, 그녀의 어머니 Alice Giffard(은)는 25세였다. 그녀는 대략 1280년 Peter le Poer(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그녀는 대략 1307년 영국 코른월 펜린에서 44세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 코른월 펜린에 묻혔다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Sybil de Mandeville foi batizada em 29 de setembro de 1264, em Luton, Bedfordshire, Inglaterra, seu pai, Walter De Mandeville, tinha 55 anos e sua mãe, Alice Giffard, tinha 25 anos. Ela casou-se com Peter le Poer aproximadamente 1280. Eles tiveram pelo menos 1 filho. Ela faleceu aproximadamente 1307, em Penryn, Cornwall, Inglaterra, com 44 anos, e foi sepultada em Penryn, Cornwall, Inglaterra.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Sybil de Mandeville была крещена 29 сентября 1264 г. в местности лутон, бедфордшир, Англия, её отцу по имени Walter De Mandeville было 55 лет, а матери, которую звали Alice Giffard, 25. Она вышла замуж за человека по имени Peter le Poer около 1280 г. У них был по крайней мере 1 сын. Она умерла около 1307 г. в местности пенрин, корнвалл, Англия в возрасте 44 лет и была похоронена в местности пенрин, корнвалл, Англия.

es.lifeSummary: Cuando Sybil de Mandeville se bautizó el 29 de septiembre de 1264, en Luton, Bedfordshire, Inglaterra, su papá, Walter De Mandeville, tenía 55 años y su mamá, Alice Giffard, tenía 25 años. Se casó con Peter le Poer aproximadamente 1280. Fueron padres de por lo menos 1 hijo. Murió aproximadamente 1307, en Penryn, Cornwall, Inglaterra, a la edad de 44 años, y fue sepultada en Penryn, Cornwall, Inglaterra.
